coaching.coaching: Pin down acceptance criteria observability and verification with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: acceptance criteria observability and verification

coaching.coaching: Plan how AC4 experiment output verification method will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: AC4 experiment output verification method

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: 4 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC4 so its verify command (or an added structured assertion) confirms the experiment's recorded output demonstrates successful criterion activation and move rendering — for example, pipe `rebar show jira-reb-1003` through a grep/jq assertion that matches a known success token in the recorded output, or explicitly tag AC4 as `[operator-attested]` (per ADR 0043) so reviewers know its evidence is a human-reviewed attestation rather than a machine-checkable command.
evidence: 'exercises criterion activation through Pass-1 fan-in and the project move through Pass-4 applicability/rendering, with the command and successful output recorded in an epic comment'
The verify command reads the ticket but does not re-run the experiment or assert on structured output — a failed experiment whose output is pasted verbatim would still pass this AC.

F1(c): the presence of a comment is a one-time transition ('was the experiment run?') rather than a durable end-state ('does criterion activation work?'); durable coverage for activation is provided by AC1, making AC4 redundant but weaker.
findings.finding: AC4's verify command (`rebar show jira-reb-1003`) only confirms that a comment exists on the ticket; it does not structurally check whether the experiment produced successful output, so the AC can be 'green' even if the recorded experiment shows errors or incomplete results.
scenarios: Agent pastes experiment output showing a routing error into the ticket comment; `rebar show` returns the comment and AC4 is marked green despite the criterion not activating correctly.
A future reader re-runs `rebar show` after the ticket is stale and cannot distinguish a successful experiment from a partial one.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) prepend `[operator-attested]` to the AC4 checkbox text so the operator-attested exception applies and the verify becomes a recorded attestation by design, or (b) add a grep assertion: `rebar show jira-reb-1003 | grep -qE 'project\.portability.*activated|experiment.*succeeded'` so the command structurally confirms success.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the architectural decision for project-scoped criteria overlays and project moves as the extension boundary (why this design, what it rules out, and how it composes with the generic kernel), separate from the client-facing how-to example.
evidence: 'Documentation of the project configuration as an example for clients' — scoped to example, not architectural rationale
'The experiment evidence and implementation documentation show client authors how the two project extension seams compose' — framed as how-to
No mention of ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry capturing the extension-boundary decision
T5e checklist: 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / CLAUDE.md / design-doc update'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — project-scoped criteria overlays and project-scoped Pass-4 moves as the sanctioned extension boundary — but the committed documentation artifact is described only as a 'client-facing example' (how-to), with no explicit commitment to capturing the design rationale (why this boundary, why not inline config, why project move vs. generic move) in an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design doc. Client authors replicating the pattern will lack the canonical reasoning when the example alone is insufficient.
findings.location: Scope section ('Documentation of the project configuration as an example for clients'); Success Criteria ('show client authors how the two project extension seams compose'); AC final bullet ('documented as a client-facing example')
scenarios: A future rebar client author wants to add a project-specific criterion but cannot find the rationale for why project overlays (not inline config) are the right seam — the how-to example tells them what files to create but not why the boundary is drawn there.

A rebar maintainer reviewing a PR that modifies criteria_routing.json cannot find the ADR that establishes the project-overlay contract, making it harder to judge whether the PR respects the intended boundary.
findings.suggested_fix: Create `.rebar/decisions/ADR-001-project-extension-seams.md` (or add an `## Extension boundary` section to `CLAUDE.md`) that records: (1) the decision to use project overlay files rather than inline or runtime configuration, (2) the rationale (blast-radius isolation, kernel stability, dogfooding), (3) the constraints (project move must list `applies_when` criterion ids; prompt must supply all four finding elements), and (4) a pointer to the dogfood example. Reference this from the client-facing example doc.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
